title: practical english usage - Part 85
content:
that are followed by -ing forms cannot usually be followed by infinitives normally used to connect the noun/adjective to the -ing form. Nouns/adjectives Some nouns and adjectives can be followed by -ing forms. A  preposition is 1  the    idea     of   getting        old;   tired      of   listening tired      of   listening                      ___________ 103 -ing    forms     after    nouns     and   adjectives: For more about structures with prepositions at the end, ►    209.

For infinitives after first, next, last or superlative + noun (e.g. the first woman to climb Everest), ►     101.3.

For for + object + infinitive (e.g. Is there any need for us to stay?), ►     113.5.

For passive infinitives (e.g. There's work to be done), ►     106.

For infinitives used to talk about people’ s purposes, ►     112.

relative clause with be (= the life/world that is to come, etc).

to be (= his future wife), the infinitive has a future meaning, and is similar to a In expressions like the life to come (= life after death), the world to come, his wife 6 the    life    to   come,      etc I need a book which fo read.

This is not possible when there is no preposition. One cannot say, for example, They're looking for a room in which to meet.

Emily needs a friend with whom to play.

whom/which + infinitive.

In a very formal style, another structure is possible: noun + preposition + Emily needs ɑ  friend to play with. They're looking for a room to meet in.

A  noun can be followed by infinitive + preposition.

5  infinitive       with    preposition:        a  friend       to   play     with grammar •  104   -ing forms after prepositions: without breaking eggs Accustomed can be followed by to + -ing form or an infinitive (►   105.11).          -* He's inclined to anger. / He's inclined to lose his temper.

She agreed to our plan. / She agreed to do what we wanted.

the infinitives of verbs. Examples are agree, consent, entitled, inclined, prone.

A  few verbs and adjectives are used with to before nouns, but are followed by I'll get round to paying the bills sooner or later.

- I'll get round to the bills sooner or later.

I prefer swimming to walking.

- I prefer the seaside to the mountains.

I'm not used to driving in London.

- I'm not used to London traffic.

Do you object to working on Sundays?

- Do you object to Sunday work?

I look forward to hearing from you. (     ... to hear from you.) - I look forward to your next email.

a noun or an -ing form.

In the following examples, note how  the preposition to can be followed by either get round to, in addition to.

forward to, object to, be used to, prefer (doing one thing to doing another), normally by the infinitive. Common expressions in which  this happens are look When  to is a preposition, it can be followed by the -ing form of a verb, but not forward to Christmas).

preposition, followed for example by a noun (e.g. She's gone to the park, I look that the next word is an infinitive (e.g. to swim, to laugh). It can also be a To is actually two different words. It can be an infinitive marker, used to show 2 to  as  a  preposition:       I look     forward         to   .. .ing not I like to cycle to work instead of driving, (     .. .-tostead to drive...) He's talking about moving to the country.

We got the job finished by working sixteen hours a day.

Always check the oil before starting the car. (     ... before to start the ear?) break-egg^) not You can't make an omelette without breaking eggs, (     .. .-without to not an infinitive.

When  we  put a verb after a preposition, we  normally use an -ing form (gerund), 1  after    all  prepositions breaking          eggs      __________________ 104 -ing    forms     after    prepositions:        without For be used to . . .ing. ►    615.

I'm proud of having won / to have won.

We have a good chance of making / to make a profit.

exceptions with adjectives).

Normally there is little or no difference of meaning (►   105.13-105.16 for some After a few nouns and adjectives, we  can use either an -ing form or an infinitive.
